does: This paper introduces Constituent-Aware Pooling (CAP), an intervention methodology that probes how transformer-based language models process compositional linguistic structures. The authors systematically analyze model activations by pooling tokens according to linguistic constituents at different layers, then evaluate how well these pooled representations perform on tasks like inverse definition modeling, hypernym prediction, and synonym identification. The key finding is that no single layer integrates tokens into cohesive semantic representations based on constituent structure; instead, the models exhibit fragmented information processing where relevant semantic information scatters across layers and token positions, with this fragmentation becoming more pronounced in larger models.
connects: This work builds on mechanistic interpretability research that investigates how neural networks implement linguistic computations, extending prior work on compositionality in neural networks and probing tasks for understanding model internals. It engages with classical compositional semantics from linguistics while applying information-theoretic analysis techniques common in recent interpretability research. The paper responds to and builds upon earlier findings about attention mechanisms and token representation, challenging assumptions that transformers learn clean compositional abstractions. It connects to ongoing debates about whether language models develop human-like syntactic and semantic hierarchies, relating to work on structure in neural representations and the inductive biases of transformer architectures.
enables: This work enables future research into designing language models with better compositional reasoning capabilities by identifying specific architectural and training limitations. It points toward interventions that could encourage more localized semantic integration at particular layers or improved constituent-based attention mechanisms. The findings suggest directions for improving model interpretability by developing better probing methods that respect linguistic structure, and for enhancing reliability by understanding why composition fails. The fragmentation pattern it identifies could inform training procedures or architectural modifications aimed at creating more transparent and systematic processing of hierarchical linguistic structures, potentially improving both interpretability and performance on compositional generalization tasks.
